scope_note: Stage One topics only. Used so the TA can exhibit plausible beginner errors.

misconceptions.description: Confuses = (assignment) with == (comparison). Uses = in conditions instead of ==.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: = assigns a value; == checks if two values are equal. In an if, we need == to compare.

misconceptions.description: Thinks range(5) produces 1 through 5, or that the end value is included.
misconceptions.example_incorrect_code:
for i in range(5):
    print(i + 1)  # expects 1,2,3,4,5 but task was 0..4
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: range(n) gives 0, 1, 2, ..., n-1. The top bound is not included.

misconceptions.description: Tries to concatenate a string and an int with + without converting (e.g., "Age: " + 20).
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: + for strings only joins two strings. In Stage One, avoid mixing a string and a number with +. Use separate print() calls for text and numbers, or teach type conversion in a later stage.

misconceptions.description: Omits or uses wrong indentation after if/else/for/while, or uses inconsistent spaces.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: After a colon, the next block must be indented (e.g., 4 spaces). All lines in that block must have the same indent.

misconceptions.description: Uses a single = in a condition or writes something like 'if x' when meaning 'if x != 0' without understanding truthiness.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: In an if we need a condition that is True or False. Use == for equality, not =.

misconceptions.description: Thinks the first element is at index 1 instead of 0.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: List indices start at 0. The first element is nums[0], the second is nums[1], and so on.

misconceptions.description: Uses print() in a function and expects the caller to get the value.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: return sends a value back to the caller; print only displays it.

misconceptions.description: Uses mutable default argument (e.g. list) in function.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: Default arguments are evaluated once; use None for mutable defaults.

misconceptions.description: Thinks loop variable disappears after the loop.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: The loop variable exists after the loop and holds the last value.

misconceptions.description: Uses a variable before assigning it or misspells name.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: Assign a value before using a variable; check spelling.

misconceptions.description: Expects / to give integer or confuses / and //.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: / is float division; // is integer (floor) division.

misconceptions.description: Confuses range(start, end) with range(end); thinks end is inclusive.
misconceptions.example_incorrect_code:
for i in range(1, 5):
    print(i)  # expects 1,2,3,4,5
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: range(a, b) goes from a to b-1; end is exclusive.

misconceptions.description: Uses else if instead of elif or wrong indentation in elif chain.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: Python uses elif, not else if; same indent as if.

misconceptions.description: Forgets to update loop variable in while, causing infinite loop.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: Update the condition variable inside the loop so it eventually becomes false.

misconceptions.description: Treats input() as returning int/float without converting.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: input() returns a string; use int() or float() for numbers.

misconceptions.description: Tries to assign to string index or slice.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: Strings are immutable; create a new string instead of modifying.

misconceptions.description: Accesses missing key with [] instead of .get().
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: Use .get(key, default) when key might be missing.

misconceptions.description: Function without return returns None; expects something else.
misconceptions.example_correct_code:
def add(a, b):
    return a + b
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: A function without return gives None; use return to send a value.

misconceptions.description: Expects both sides of and/or to always be evaluated.
misconceptions.example_incorrect_code: x = 0 and f()  # expects f() to run
misconceptions.example_correct_code: x = 0 and f()  # f() not called
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: and/or short-circuit; right side may not run.

misconceptions.description: Expects negative % positive to be negative.
misconceptions.remediation_hint: Teach: In Python, result of % has the sign of the divisor.
